description: User voice preferences learned from tweet drafting and editing. Read before drafting, append after posting.

entries.observation: Prefers direct, factual tone. No fluff, no hype.
Avoids em dashes and dashes as punctuation. Use commas, periods, or restructure instead.
Likes data-driven posts with visual elements (bar charts, lists, structured numbers).
Prefers threads over single tweets for complex topics.
Wants sources credited at the end of a tweet or thread.
Tone: informative, not preachy or political.

When describing a pain point, use the user's actual experience, not a technical description. E.g., 'stuck browsing JSONL files' was wrong, the real issue was 'can't find it in the /resume list because sessions are untitled'.
For announcement threads about personal projects, user wants to explain the 'why' (pain point) before the 'what' (solution), and give credit/context for tools used.

When asked to draft a thread about a debugging story, user countered with 'one tweet, no thread' and accepted a single-tweet condensed version. When the story can plausibly fit in one tweet (<270 chars), draft single-tweet first and offer thread as an alternative, rather than defaulting to thread for technical/multi-act stories.

For announcements about gws, the user wants to introduce it as 'gws (my unofficial Google Workspace CLI)' so unfamiliar readers immediately know what it is. Don't assume audience knows the project name.

For gws release announcements, the user accepts a tight bullet-list single tweet: header line ('gws vX.Y.Z (my unofficial Google Workspace CLI):'), one bullet per shipped feature with key flag/behavior names inlined as `code`, then the GitHub release URL on its own line. Approved on v1.37.0.

User accepts longer threads when content is dense and substantive. After approving a 4-part thread, asked to drop region-specific examples (e.g. Israel-specific DST mention) and pack each tweet closer to the 280-char limit rather than leaving slack. Treat 270-279 as the target ceiling per part when packing, not 240-260.

OPERATIONAL: never pass tweet text containing apostrophes through nested shell escapes (e.g. python3 -c 'print(...)' inside $()). Single quotes inside the inner python triple-quoted string collide with the outer shell '\''-style escaping and render as triple apostrophes in the published tweet (yesterday's became yesterday'''s). ALWAYS write each tweet to a tempfile via Write, then post with $(cat /tmp/tweet_N.txt). Cost a delete-and-repost cycle once.

Plugin breaking-change announcements use the same tight-bullet format as gws release tweets: header line with project name + parenthetical describing it ('my Claude Code plugin for X'), bullets with code-fenced flags/commands, URL on its own line. Approved on x v2.0.0 announcement. Pattern transfers across projects, not just gws.

OPERATIONAL: post.py's pre-flight uses raw len(text), NOT X's URL-weighted count. The 23-char URL discount only applies on X's server side. So a 274/280 X-adjusted tweet with a 60-char GitHub URL (raw 314) gets rejected by post.py before it ever leaves. Always count by raw len() when targeting the 270-279 ceiling, treating URLs at full length.

For technical-discovery announcement threads (TIL framing): user wants the workflow/use-case stated FIRST, before the trick. Initial draft led with the discovery, then trick, then bolted on a generic 'use case' tweet — rejected. Approved version flipped to: tweet 1 = concrete user workflow (you're in Claude Code, you want to share with team) + the surprise hook, tweet 2 = the technical trick, tweet 3 = release line + URL. Lesson: lead with the human problem the trick solves, not with the trick itself.

When the lead sentence already describes what the project IS (e.g., 'used my own tmux save daemon to recover'), the project-intro parenthetical from the 2026-04-28 pattern becomes redundant — `tmux-resurrect-launchd now has:` was approved without a `(my macOS launchd job for tmux state)` glossing. Rule: parenthetical is for when the project name appears cold; if the lead already explains the category, skip it.

Dogfooding-led-to-shipping framing works as a single-tweet lead: `Mac crashed. used my own tmux save daemon to recover and immediately found bugs. tmux-resurrect-launchd now has: …`. The crash → dogfood → bugs → ship arc is a tight 3-clause lead. Variant of the 2026-04-01 'why before what' rule, specifically for tweets about the author's own tools where the author was also the customer.

Avoid version numbers that don't formally exist. Initial draft said 'tmux-resurrect-launchd v2:' but there are no version tags in the repo — calling it v2 informally read as hype. Replaced with 'now has:'. Rule: only use version numbers in tweets when they correspond to actual git tags / GitHub releases.

For PR/release tweets about own-tools, perf-only framing is incomplete. User edited twice to add (a) what the tool does for them in plain terms (transcribe hebrew, diarization), then (b) the end-user workflow that delights (drop mp4 in folder, magic happens). Lesson: tool-announcement tweets should include the workflow/use-case, not just the perf delta. The 7hr→30min number is the punchline, but the 'drop in folder, transcripts appear' detail is what makes the tweet land.

Prefer plain-English equivalents over jargon when both fit. 'transcripts with speaker labels' was approved over 'transcripts + speaker diarization' in the same context. Diarization is the technical term; speaker labels is what the reader actually sees. Rule: if a non-jargon synonym fits the char budget, use it — tweets serve a broader audience than a PR description.

Lowercase opening sentences are accepted (`gemini in google meet can't do hebrew`). User did not capitalize Gemini, Google Meet, or sentence-initial words during edits. This is a stylistic stance, not a typo. Match it: prefer lowercase prose throughout the tweet body unless a proper noun is genuinely ambiguous in lowercase.

For flag-feature bullets, prefer a concrete example with recognizable values over abstract syntax. Initial draft used `--model role=name` (placeholder syntax); user asked for a use case and accepted `--model qa=haiku,cto=gpt-5` (real role IDs + real model aliases). Rule: when a bullet describes a flag, show one realistic invocation rather than the parameter shape, even at the cost of a few extra chars.

When a project sits on top of someone else's tool, credit the upstream maintainer's @-handle inside the parenthetical: `(my agent team launcher on AMQ by @avivsinai)`. Not a separate sentence, not a hashtag, not a thanks line at the end — folded into the same parenthetical that introduces the project. Pattern complements the 2026-04-28 'introduce the project for cold readers' rule for projects that build on third-party tools.
